workshops.title: Own Your Digital Presence
workshops.description: Structure your site, social, and email list so your practice is findable—without surrendering your voice to platforms.
outcomes: Clarify who the site is for and what they should do next
Draft information architecture (pages) and a sustainable update rhythm
Compare tradeoffs of rented platforms vs an owned web presence
workshops.content: We map audience, tone, and infrastructure: domain, simple site architecture, accessibility basics, and sustainable rhythms for updates. You leave with a prioritized checklist, draft copy blocks, and clear next steps for the next 30 days.

metadata.subtitle: Portfolio clarity, owned infrastructure, and humane habits for showing work online.
metadata.shortDescription: A structured pass through site architecture, copy, and presence for working artists.
metadata.seoTitle: Own Your Digital Presence — Oolite Arts
metadata.seoDescription: Plan your artist site, socials, and updates with a checklist you can actually maintain.
materialsRequired: Links to existing site or socials if any

agenda.title: Audience & goals
agenda.summary: Who you are speaking to and what a successful visit looks like.

agenda.title: Site map & page purposes
agenda.summary: Every page answers one question; trim dead weight.

agenda.title: Voice, access, and maintenance
agenda.summary: Tone, alt text basics, and a rhythm you can keep.

instructors.name: Professional Development
instructors.title: Oolite Arts
instructors.bio: Focuses on sustainable careers for artists in nonprofit and public contexts.

faq.q: I only have Instagram—is that ok?
faq.a: Yes. We map what to keep and what to add so audiences can go deeper.

whoFor: Artists building or refreshing a public-facing site
Educators who need shared language for cohort digital presence
whoNotFor: Teams needing a full rebrand in one afternoon
Advanced technical SEO audits—we cover foundations only
metadata.placeholderImagePrompt: Editorial photo: artist at laptop, soft daylight, minimal desk, subtle Miami pastel wall, no logos, candid not stock-smile
metadata.packetConcept: A 12-page facilitator packet: learning goals, timing blocks, slide outline, copy worksheet (hero, about, contact), platform comparison one-pager, and a 30-day maintenance calendar template.

workshops.title: Learn AI Without Losing Yourself
workshops.description: A facilitator-led workshop on using AI tools with clarity, boundaries, and voice intact—pressure, prompts, problems, and practice in one session.
outcomes: Name where AI helps vs where human judgment must stay
Run a short critique loop on AI-assisted drafts without losing voice
Leave with a one-page “use policy” you can share with collaborators
Map one real workflow from prompt to publication with guardrails
prerequisites: Willingness to try one browser-based AI tool (free tier ok)
workshops.content: This workshop treats AI as infrastructure you steer—not a replacement author. We work through pressure (what the room feels), prompts (how you ask), problems (what actually needs solving), and practice (repeatable loops you can reuse). Bring a real project or admin task; leave with language for grants, collaborators, and your future self.

metadata.subtitle: Editorial, human-centered AI literacy—voice, boundaries, and repeatable practice.
metadata.shortDescription: Pressure, prompt, problem, and practice: a single session that keeps authorship visible and workflows sustainable.
materialsRequired: Account on one public AI chat tool (free tier ok)

agenda.title: Pressure / Prompt / Problem / Practice
agenda.summary: Four moves with live examples and pair prompts.

agenda.title: Scenarios and differentiation
agenda.summary: Table reads; when automation is appropriate vs not.

instructors.name: Moises Sanabria
instructors.title: Digital practice
instructors.bio: Facilitation focused on critical tool use, documentation, and artist-centered workflows.

whoFor: Artists and educators new to structured AI use
Teams setting norms for disclosure and critique
whoNotFor: Uncritical tool hype or vendor-specific certification prep

workshops.title: SEO for Artists in the Age of AI Search
workshops.description: Foundational SEO when summaries and chat-style answers change how people discover culture—language, structure, and credibility without gimmicks.
outcomes: Align page titles and descriptions with real search intent
Apply structured thinking to events and programs pages
Recognize SEO and AI-search advice that does not fit nonprofit arts work
prerequisites: An existing site or project URL to review
workshops.content: Search is part of cultural literacy. We cover how people look for programs and artists today, how to write titles and descriptions that match intent, and how to spot opaque growth advice that does not fit arts contexts. Includes a short segment on AI overviews and what still belongs on your own pages.

metadata.subtitle: Ethical, practical discoverability for artists and small cultural orgs.
metadata.shortDescription: Intent, structure, and credibility—search basics that respect arts contexts and new AI interfaces.
materialsRequired: CMS or site access if possible

agenda.title: How discovery works in arts contexts
Workshop: rewrite titles & descriptions
agenda.summary: Peer review with a simple rubric.

agenda.title: Q&A and responsible metrics
agenda.summary: What to track without losing the plot.

instructors.name: Digital Lab team
instructors.bio: Supports public-facing digital literacy across Oolite programming.

faq.a: We focus on organic fundamentals; ads are optional context only.

whoFor: Small teams publishing programs and open calls
metadata.placeholderImagePrompt: Abstract diagram aesthetic: search results morphing into chat bubble, muted teal and coral, museum-quality typography, no brand marks
metadata.packetConcept: Facilitator guide with AI-search vs classic SERP comparison sheet, rewrite exercises for three page types (bio, exhibition, program), and a metrics appendix focused on meaningful referrals—not vanity counts.

workshops.title: Writing About Your Digital Practice
workshops.description: Turn process, tools, and URLs into clear language for statements, panels, and funders—without sounding like a press release.
outcomes: Produce a draft artist statement paragraph that names medium and audience
Describe technical process in plain language for non-specialist readers
Align writing with documentation you already have or will collect
prerequisites: Examples of recent work (images, links, or files)
workshops.content: We work from artifacts: screenshots, repos, links, and studio notes. You draft short paragraphs that explain what the work is, how it was made, and why it matters now. Emphasis on honest authorship when tools include AI and platforms.

metadata.subtitle: Statements, bios, and panel language that match how you actually work.
metadata.shortDescription: From studio notes and links to clear writing for grants, sites, and public talks.

agenda.title: From artifact to paragraph
agenda.summary: Pair exercises from URLs and images.

agenda.title: Authorship and tools
agenda.summary: Naming collaboration and AI use clearly.

instructors.name: Professional Development
instructors.title: Oolite Arts
instructors.bio: Supports artists translating studio practice into public language.

faq.q: Do I need a finished website?
faq.a: No—links, docs, or files are enough to start.

whoFor: Artists preparing grants, residencies, or public talks
Educators coaching cohorts on artist statements
whoNotFor: Fiction or poetry workshops—this is about discursive practice writing
metadata.placeholderImagePrompt: Close-up of handwritten notes next to keyboard, warm light, creative mess, no readable private text
metadata.packetConcept: Printable scaffolds: one-page bios (50/100/250 words), process paragraph templates, glossary of common digital terms for funders, and panel prep question bank.

workshops.title: Documentation for Artists
workshops.description: Capture installation, performance, and time-based work so archives, press, and future-you can understand what happened.
outcomes: Write a one-page documentation plan for your next show or activation
Choose formats and resolutions appropriate for web, print, and archive
Apply a simple naming and backup habit to new files
workshops.content: We cover capture plans, lighting and color basics, file naming, consent when people appear, and what to hand off to an archivist or org. You leave with a shoot checklist and a folder structure you can reuse.

metadata.subtitle: Checklists, file habits, and ethics for documenting live and installed work.
metadata.shortDescription: From capture plan to folder structure—documentation that scales from studio to archive.
materialsRequired: Optional: camera or recent project media

agenda.title: Consent and collaboration
agenda.summary: People in the frame; credit lines.

instructors.name: Digital Lab team
instructors.title: Oolite Arts
instructors.bio: Supports documentation and public-facing media for artists.

faq.a: No—phones and available light are enough for the habits we teach.

whoFor: Artists who document their own work
metadata.placeholderImagePrompt: Gallery interior, tripod and camera in soft focus, neutral walls, documentary not glamorous
metadata.packetConcept: Shot list templates for install, performance, and public program; model release notes; ingest checklist for orgs; and a one-page 'minimum viable documentation' for tight budgets.

workshops.title: AI for Artists: Voice, Workflow, and Authorship
workshops.description: Use AI tools deliberately in studio and admin workflows while keeping voice, credit, and ethics visible.
outcomes: Draft a short personal AI-use policy for grants and collaborators
Run a structured critique session on AI-assisted drafts
Identify three workflow steps where human judgment must stay in the loop
prerequisites: Comfort trying a browser-based AI tool
workshops.content: We separate experimentation from publication, build personal policies for disclosure, and map where AI saves time vs where it erases judgment. Includes hands-on prompts for research, drafting, and iteration—not a single-tool tutorial tied to one vendor.

metadata.subtitle: Policies, prompts, and critique—AI as studio assistant, not author replacement.
metadata.shortDescription: Practical frameworks for voice, disclosure, and workflow when AI is in the room.
materialsRequired: Account on one public AI chat tool (free tier ok)

agenda.title: Workflow mapping
agenda.summary: Where AI enters; where it must not.

instructors.name: Digital Lab team
instructors.title: Oolite Arts
instructors.bio: Supports critical digital practice and tool literacy.

faq.q: Is this only for visual artists?
faq.a: No—writers and interdisciplinary practitioners are welcome.

whoNotFor: Legal advice— we give frameworks, not counsel
metadata.placeholderImagePrompt: Split composition: human hand sketching beside abstract neural motif, editorial illustration style, restrained palette
metadata.packetConcept: Sample policies (short/medium), facilitator notes on common objections, bibliography of arts-AI readings, and a peer critique rubric for AI-assisted writing and images.

workshops.title: Vibe-coding with Net Art
workshops.description: Use contemporary AI-assisted workflows to sketch, prototype, and publish small net art pieces—without losing authorship or critical intent.
outcomes: Ship a small browser-based sketch or micro-site
Document prompts, edits, and authorship choices
Connect the work to net art histories and contemporary practice
workshops.content: This workshop treats vibe-coding as a disciplined sketch process: short cycles, clear constraints, and documentation of decisions. You leave with a micro-site or interactive sketch and language to talk about it in portfolio and grant contexts.

metadata.subtitle: Creative coding meets critical publishing—fast iteration, clear intent.
metadata.shortDescription: Prototype net art with AI-assisted workflows while keeping authorship visible.

instructors.name: Digital Lab team
instructors.title: Oolite Arts
instructors.bio: Supports artists working at the intersection of studio practice and digital infrastructure.

testimonials.quote: I finally had language for what I was doing with AI tools—and a file I could show.

faq.q: Do I need to know how to code?
faq.a: Some HTML/CSS comfort helps; we start from templates and focus on iteration and documentation.
Publishing is optional; you control what you share during the session.

whoFor: Artists curious about creative coding and the web as medium
Studio practitioners who want publishable artifacts quickly
whoNotFor: Those seeking a full-stack engineering bootcamp
Participants who need a fully offline workflow only
metadata.placeholderImagePrompt: Browser window with abstract generative art, terminal snippet blurred, dark mode aesthetic, arts context not corporate
metadata.packetConcept: Starter repo README, history-of-net-art reading links (short), documentation worksheet for prompts/commits, deploy checklist (static host).

workshops.title: Organizing Your Digital Studio
workshops.description: Folders, backups, passwords, and project handoffs—so your digital studio stays legible to you and collaborators.
outcomes: Adopt a repeatable project folder template
Choose a backup approach appropriate to your risk tolerance
Document one collaboration handoff (what a partner receives)
workshops.content: We build a lightweight system: project skeletons, naming, cloud vs local tradeoffs, version habits for images and video, and a simple security baseline. Designed for artists who work across many small projects, not enterprise IT.

metadata.subtitle: Files, backups, and handoffs—systems that survive busy studio seasons.
metadata.shortDescription: Templates and habits for digital projects without enterprise IT overhead.
materialsRequired: List of current storage tools you use

agenda.title: Template build
agenda.summary: Apply skeleton to a real project.

instructors.name: Digital Lab team
instructors.title: Oolite Arts
instructors.bio: Supports sustainable digital infrastructure for artists.

faq.a: Both—patterns are tool-agnostic with OS-specific tips.

whoFor: Artists juggling many concurrent digital projects
metadata.placeholderImagePrompt: Neat folder icons as subtle abstract shapes, paper texture overlay, calm green-gray palette
metadata.packetConcept: Printable folder tree diagrams, password-manager onboarding checklist, collaborator handoff one-pager, and optional Obsidian/Drive comparison for artists.

workshops.title: AI, Copyright, and Creative Risk
workshops.description: A practical overview of how copyright, contracts, and platform terms intersect with AI-assisted art—what to watch for, not legal advice.
outcomes: Identify contract and terms clauses that touch AI-generated or assisted work
Separate legal unknowns from studio risk decisions you control
Draft questions for counsel or institutional partners
workshops.content: We map common scenarios: training data debates, terms of use for tools, gallery and festival agreements, and how to talk to collaborators about disclosure. You leave with questions to ask a lawyer and clearer language for your own policies.

metadata.subtitle: Contracts, terms, and studio risk—framed for artists and small orgs.
metadata.shortDescription: Navigate AI and IP conversations with clearer vocabulary and safer habits—not a substitute for legal counsel.
